title: if / else — the basic decision
instruction:
You know what if/else does — but JavaScript's version has a few quirks worth knowing.

The braces `{}` are technically optional for single-line bodies, but always use them. Missing braces cause famous bugs (Apple's 'goto fail' SSL vulnerability was exactly this).

Run this, then change the score to see each path.
successMessage: 78 → C. Each condition is checked top-to-bottom. The first one that's true wins — everything below is skipped.

title: Logical operators: &&, ||, !
instruction:
Combine conditions:

- `&&` (AND) — both must be true
- `||` (OR) — at least one must be true
- `!` (NOT) — flip true/false

Write the access check: grant access if user is an admin OR (is verified AND is at least 18).

codeChecks.message: Use && to combine verified and age.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: isAdmin was false but (isVerified && age >= 20) was true, so access granted. Parentheses make the logic explicit — always add them when mixing && and ||.

title: Short-circuit evaluation
instruction:
JavaScript evaluates `&&` and `||` lazily — it stops as soon as it knows the answer:

- `false && anything` → stops at false, returns `false`
- `true || anything` → stops at true, returns `true`

This is used constantly to write concise conditional code:

```js
user && console.log(user.name)  // only runs if user exists
name || "Anonymous"             // fallback if name is falsy
```

Run this.
successMessage: Short-circuit evaluation makes conditions concise. user && doSomething() is so common it has its own name: 'guard clause'. You'll write this pattern constantly.

title: ?? — nullish coalescing
instruction:
`||` has a problem: it treats ALL falsy values as 'no value'. That means `0 || 100` gives 100 — even though 0 is a valid score.

`??` (nullish coalescing) only falls back when the left side is `null` or `undefined`:

```js
0 ?? 100    → 0    (0 is not null/undefined)
0 || 100   → 100  (0 is falsy)
```

Fix the defaults to use `??` instead of `||`.
hint: Replace || with ?? for the score and count

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: ?? vs || — this distinction matters enormously. Use ?? when 0 and "" are valid values. Use || only when you want ALL falsy values to trigger the fallback.

title: ?. — optional chaining
instruction:
Accessing a property on `null` or `undefined` crashes with a TypeError. Optional chaining `?.` returns `undefined` instead:

```js
user?.address?.city   // undefined if user or address is null
```

This replaced dozens of lines of defensive null checks in modern JavaScript.
successMessage: ?. is a modern JavaScript superpower. Before it existed, you'd write: user && user.address && user.address.city. Now it's just user?.address?.city. Game changer.

title: Ternary — one-line if/else
instruction:
The ternary operator is a compact if/else that returns a value:

```js
condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
```

Use it for simple assignments. Don't nest ternaries — it becomes unreadable fast.

Rewrite the four-line if/else as a single ternary.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: One line, same result. Ternaries are everywhere in JSX/React: {isLoggedIn ? <UserMenu /> : <LoginButton />}. Keep them simple — one condition only.

title: switch — match exact values
instruction:
`switch` is cleaner than a long if/else chain when matching exact values.

Important: always `break` at the end of each case, or execution 'falls through' to the next case!

Add cases for `'pause'` and `'resume'`.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: switch with break — never forget the break, or cases bleed into each other. Intentional fall-through (multiple cases, same action) is a valid pattern but should be commented.

title: Logical assignment operators
starter:
let config = { timeout: 0, retries: null, debug: true };

config.timeout ||= 30;   // 0 is falsy, so this sets it to 30
config.retries ??= 3;    // null, so this sets it to 3  
config.debug &&= false;  // debug is truthy, so this sets it to false

console.log(config.timeout);  // 30
console.log(config.retries);  // 3
console.log(config.debug);    // false
successMessage: Logical assignment operators reduce boilerplate dramatically. ??= is especially useful for initialising optional config values — set it only when truly absent.

title: Build an access control system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build `checkAccess(user)` that returns a message based on these rules:
- If user is null: `"No user — please log in."`
- If user.isBanned: `"Account banned."`
- If user.role === 'admin': `"Admin access granted."`
- If user.isVerified && user.age >= 18: `"Access granted."`
- Otherwise: `"Access denied."`

Test it with 5 different user objects covering each case.
hint: Use ?. and ?? for safe access. Use if/else if chain for the rules.
successMessage: A complete access control system using &&, ||, ??, ?., and if/else. All of Chapter 5 in one function. This is how real auth middleware works. Chapter 5 done!
